ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for grilled spiced chicken; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for marinated grilled chicken does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Chicken meat, grilled) is the closest defensible match. The spiced marinade component (chilli, garlic, shallot, ginger, turmeric) is not captured in a single FoodEx2 term; the base preparation code is used with this note.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for grilled spiced chicken found in the clinical terminology hierarchy; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled spiced chicken is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one medium-sized piece of skinless chicken, as commonly served in a Singaporean hawker or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Spiced Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Grilled Spiced Chicken (CR ID F11020035), which is a canonical Singapore food composition record. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.2 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for skinless grilled chicken thigh/breast composite (USDA SR Legacy Items 05064 and 05013) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: N/A — Grilled Spiced Chicken contains negligible carbohydrates (0.3 g/100g)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Carbohydrate content is 0.3 g/100g, which is negligible. A serving of 120 g yields approximately 0.36 g of carbohydrate, producing a glycemic load of approximately 0.1, which is clinically insignificant. GI testing is not meaningful for foods with negligible carbohydrate content per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 167 mg/100g is borderline; a 120 g serving contributes approximately 200 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid adding salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium condiments to the marinade, as cumulative sodium from multiple servings or accompaniments can exceed the recommended 2000 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.3 g/100g with minimal sugar (0.2 g/100g) and high protein (20.4 g/100g), producing a glycemic load near zero
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled spiced chicken is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as its negligible carbohydrate content does not raise postprandial blood glucose; however, patients should ensure no sweet marinades (honey, sugar-based sauces) are added, which would increase glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at approximately 3.2 g/100g, total fat is 12.6 g/100g (predominantly unsaturated from skinless chicken), and no trans fat is present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled skinless spiced chicken is a heart-healthy protein choice; the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) may confer additional cardiovascular benefit, but sodium at 167 mg/100g should be monitored in patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice marinade (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides curcumin, gingerols, and polyphenols; saturated fat is low at approximately 3.2 g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled spiced chicken is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and low saturated fat; patients with nightshade sensitivity should note that chilli is a nightshade-derived ingredient and may wish to monitor their individual response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Spiced Chicken is a Singaporean and Southeast Asian dish consisting of skinless chicken pieces marinated in a blend of ground chilli, garlic, shallot, ginger, and fresh turmeric, then cooked over a grill or open flame. The marinade imparts a vibrant golden-orange colour from the turmeric, with a warm, aromatic, mildly spicy flavour profile and a slightly charred, smoky exterior. The texture is firm and juicy when properly grilled, with the spice crust providing a fragrant, slightly crisp surface. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (20.4 g/100g) and moderate in fat (12.6 g/100g), with negligible carbohydrates (0.3 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and high-protein dietary patterns. Saturated fat is low at approximately 3.2 g/100g. Sodium is 167 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The spice blend — particularly turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), and garlic (allicin) — provides anti-inflammatory and antioxidant phytochemicals that may benefit individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a main protein dish in Singaporean hawker centres, home kitchens, and Malay-style restaurants, often accompanied by steamed rice, roti, or vegetables. The dish is known in Malay as Ayam Bakar Berempah and in Chinese as 香料烤鸡.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11020035), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. The match is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for skinless grilled chicken as HPB does not separately report this sub-fraction; this is documented in notes and nutrient_source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for skinless grilled chicken (composite of breast and thigh, items 05064 and 05013) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This is a reasonable approximation given the skinless preparation. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific grilled spiced chicken preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F11020035, and no sufficiently close analogue with full micronutrient data is available in USDA FoodData Central for this spiced preparation as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (F11020035) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (accessed 2026-04-16). 3. Glycemic index is not applicable: carbohydrate content is 0.3 g/100g, which is negligible. GI testing is not meaningful for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per Veritas scoring rules. Glycemic load per 120 g serving is approximately 0.1, which is clinically insignificant. 4. The input record referenced 'cinnamon' in the diabetes health note, but cinnamon is not listed in the food description or ingredient list; this reference has been removed as unsupported. 5. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.477 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced with null/not_applicable per Veritas q4 rules, as the source is not an approved GI source and GI is not applicable for this food. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 (chicken dish) used as closest_parent match; FoodEx2 A00GJ (Chicken meat, grilled) used as closest_parent match. No SNOMED CT code assigned. Match types corrected to use only approved values: 'closest_parent', 'exact', or 'none'.
